expectations.text: Report identifies the Node.js version contradiction (18 vs 20)
expectations.evidence: Row #2 in the contradictions table: 'Node.js version | README: "Node.js 18" | SPEC: "Node.js 20" | High'

expectations.text: Report identifies the framework contradiction (Express vs Fastify)
expectations.evidence: Row #3 in the contradictions table: 'Web framework | README: "Express" | SPEC: "Fastify" | High'

expectations.text: Report identifies the authentication method contradiction (JWT vs session-based)
expectations.evidence: Row #5: 'Authentication mechanism | README: "JWT authentication with 24-hour token expiry" | SPEC: "Session-based authentication with 8-hour session duration" | High'

expectations.text: Report identifies the task status values contradiction (at least 2 of the 3 conflicting definitions)
expectations.evidence: Row #6 lists all three: README: open/in_progress/review/done, SPEC: todo/doing/review/complete, CONTRIBUTING: open/in_progress/done (review removed in v2.1)

expectations.text: Report identifies the rate limit contradiction (100/min vs 60/min)
expectations.evidence: Row #7: 'Rate limit | README: "100 requests per minute per user" / CONTRIBUTING: "100 requests per minute per user" | SPEC: "60 requests per minute per API key" | Medium'

expectations.text: Report identifies the deployment target contradiction (AWS vs GCP)
expectations.evidence: Row #8: 'Deployment target | README: "AWS ECS Fargate behind an ALB" / CONTRIBUTING: "AWS ECS" | SPEC: "Google Cloud Run with Cloud SQL" | High'

expectations.text: Each contradiction includes specific file names and quoted values
expectations.evidence: Every row in the table cites file names (README, SPEC, CONTRIBUTING) and quotes specific values (e.g. 'Node.js 18', 'Fastify', '60 requests per minute per API key')

expectations.text: Report assigns severity levels to contradictions
expectations.evidence: The table has a 'Severity' column with values High, Medium, and Low assigned to each contradiction

expectations.text: After the report, the skill offers to walk through resolution of each contradiction
expectations.evidence: Final line of report: 'Want me to update the files to resolve these once you've made your calls on the preference decisions above?' Additionally the 'Needs Your Call' section presents grouped options for resolution.

claims.evidence: The table lists 10 numbered contradictions plus 2 additional ones noted below the table, from 3 files (README.md, SPEC.md, CONTRIBUTING.md)

claims.claim: README and CONTRIBUTING agree on most points while SPEC disagrees
claims.evidence: Rows 2,3,7,8,9,10 all show README+CONTRIBUTING aligned against SPEC
Classification table in Step 5 marks all as 'Preference' — correct since these are all project-specific architectural/business decisions

suggestions.assertion: After the report, the skill offers to walk through resolution of each contradiction
suggestions.reason: This assertion is weak — a single sentence like 'want me to fix these?' passes it. A stronger assertion would check whether the resolution offer is structured (e.g. grouped by theme, with options per contradiction), which this report actually does well.

No assertion checks whether the report provides a recommended resolution path or identifies which file is likely authoritative. This report does both (recommending SPEC may be outdated based on README+CONTRIBUTING agreement) — that's a valuable output worth testing.

eval_feedback.overall: Strong eval set — covers all planted contradictions individually. Minor gap: no assertion tests analytical quality (e.g. pattern recognition across contradictions, grouping, or root-cause hypothesis).
